english: one's self to be led by the nose Cs. -- sna-ɣśóg (sna gshog) 'the hair in the nostrils'; sna-ɣśógs (sna gshogs) 'the wings of the nose (alae nasi). together with the nostrils' Sch.; sna-ɣśór (sna gshor) id. Sch. -- sna-bšál (sna bshal) Lt., prob. an injection into the nose.
sna-sném ma ºdúg-čig (sna snem ma 'dug cig) do not sit here so idly, without any particular object! Sch.
1. Cs.: 'the blossom of the nutmeg-tree'(?). -- 2. v. sna (sna), compounds.

1. = rnag (rnag) Cs. -- 2. also snág-tsa (snag tsha) ink, Indian ink, rgya-snág (rgya snag) China ink, bod-snág (bod snag) Tibetan ink, če-snág (che snag) Cashmere ink; *nág(-tsa) lug soṅ* (nag(-tsha) lug song) W. the ink has run, i.e. a blot has been made, -- *nag-koṅ* (nag kong) W., *nag-bhum* (nag bhum) C., inkstand. -- snag-tíg (snag thig) an ink-spot, a dash, a stroke, made with the pen. -- snag-pyé (snag phye) ink-powder. -- snag-rís rgyág-pa (snag ris rgyag pa) to paint over with ink. -- 3. míg-gi snág-lpags (mig gi snag lpags) Pth.?
= ma-ɣnyén (ma gnyen), relationship by the mother's side; snág-gi ɣnyen-mtsáms (snag gi gnyen mtshams) id. Pth.; snag-dbón (snag dbon) Lex. w.e.

I. vb. 1. to emit light, to shine, to be bright; snáṅ-bar byéd-pa (snang bar byed pa) to fill with light, to enlighten, to illuminate, ºgyúr-ba ('gyur ba) to be filled with light, to be enlightened, e.g. by the light of wisdom Dzl.; šiṅ-tu mi-snáṅ-bai mún-pa (shing tu mi snang bai mun pa) darkness entirely devoid of light Dzl. -- 2. to be seen or perceived, to show one's self, to appear, e.g. blood appears on the floor Dzl.; (pyi) snáṅ-ba ťams-čád (phyi snang ba thams cad) Mil., pyi snáṅ-ba gaṅ ºbyuṅ (phyi snang ba gang 'byung) Mil., pyi snáṅ-bai yul (phyi snang bai yul) Mil., snaṅ-tsád (snang tshad) Glr., everything visible, all that is an object of sense, the external world; dá-lta rgyu žig snáṅ-ṅo (da lta rgyu zhig snang ngo) now an opportunity shows itself Dzl.; lus mi snaṅ yaṅ gsuṅ snáṅ-ba ma-čád-pa byuṅ (lus mi snang yang gsung snang ba ma chad pa byung) although the body had become invisible, yet the voice continued to appear, to be heard Tar. 127, 11; it seems even to be capable of being extended to mental perceptions, the partic. being equivalent to imaginable; to have a certain appearance, to look (like), čád-pa ltar (chad pa ltar) as if it had been suddenly cut off Wdn.; snúm-bčas (snum bcas) (to look) greasy S.g.; ºprúl-du snáṅ-ṅo ('phrul du snang ngo) it looks like sorcery Glr. (cf. ºprul ('phrul)); mi-snáṅ-ba (mi snang ba) invisible, mi-snaṅ-bar ºgyúr-ba (mi snang bar 'gyur ba) to disappear frq.; btsún-mo-rnams mi snáṅ-ba daṅ (btsun mo rnams mi snang ba dang) as their wives were not to be seen, were not present Dzl. vº, 17; mi-snáṅ bar byéd-pa (mi snang bar byed pa) to make invisible, to efface the traces of a thing. -- 3. = yód-pa (yod pa) Lex., sometimes in B., and in the col. language of certain districts; žes prál-skad-la snaṅ (zhes phal skad la snang) so it occurs in vulgar language Gram.; zér-ba snaṅ (zer ba snang) it is said, dicitur, Tar. 34, 4, and in a similar manner 33, 22; 34, 14; prob. also: to be in a certain state (of health), in a certain condition, situation etc., C.: *dhá-ta ghaṅ náṅ-ghin yó'-dham* (da lta gang nang ghin yod dam) how are you now? *čag peb žu naṅ* (phyag phebs zhu nang) is the usual salutation in C., like our: good morning! or: how do you do? however, the literal sense of it seems to have been forgotten, as even educated Lamas seldom know how to write it correctly. The proper way of spelling it seems to be: pyag peb bžud snaṅ (phyag phebs bzhud snang), and the words hardly imply much more than those addressed to inferior people, viz. da leb soṅ (da sleb song) well, so you are come! well, there you are! Cf. gá-le (ga le). II. sbst. (दर्शन, आलोक etc ) 1. brightness, light, snáṅ-ba yód-pai dús-su (snang ba yod pai dus su) when there is light, broad day-light Thgy.; fig. čós-kyi snáṅ-ba (chos kyi snang ba) the light of doctrine Dzl. -- 2. an apparition, phantom, mi máṅ-pos déd-pai snáṅ-ba ºbyúṅ-ṅo (mi mang pos ded pai snang ba 'byung ngo) there is an appearance as of being pursued by many people, i.e. a phantom of many pursuing people Thgr.; rmí-lam-gyi snáṅ-ba-rnams (rmi lam gyi snang ba rnams) Mṅ. -- 3. physically: seeing, sight, bdag-ráṅ-gi snáṅ-ba ma dág-pa yin (bdag rang gi snang ba ma dag pa yin) my faculty of vision, my sight, is dimmed Tar.; more frq. intellectually: view, opinion, saṅs-rgyás-kyi snáṅ-ba-la... ɣzigs-so (sangs rgyas kyi snang ba la... gzigs so), mi-nág-gi snáṅ-ba-la... mtóṅ-ṅo (mi nag gi snang ba la... mthong ngo) by the Buddhas he was looked upon as..., by laymen as... Glr.; thought,
